texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A method was developed for selection of promising solvents based on CO2 absorption experiments at 40 ◦ C and 9.5 kPa CO2 partial pressure followed by desorption of the same solvents at 80 ◦ C down to 1.0 kPa CO2 partial pressure. Experiments conducted on 13 solvent systems under atmospheric conditions revealed the solvents absorption and desorption characteristics and these were compared with 1.0 M, 2.5 M, 5.0 M and 10.0 M MEA. Results showed that absorption or stripping data alone were not sufficient in making robust solvent selection decisions, and that combined data analysis was necessary. 1.0 M tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA) and 5.0 M MEA showed the best performance in terms of absorption rate. 1.5 M Bis-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) amine (TMBPA) was easy to desorb, has high absorption capacity; and when promoted it showed the best performance in terms of CO2 carrying capacity. At the test conditions, 1.5 M TMBPA promoted with 1.0 M PZ showed the best potential for efficient CO2 removal at reduced cost of all systems tested. Its cyclic capacity in mol CO2/mol amine was found to be 70% higher than that of 5 M MEA.
texts.text: A method was developed for selection of promising solvents based on CO2 absorption experiments at 40 ◦ C and 9.5 kPa CO2 partial pressure followed by desorption of the same solvents at 80 ◦ C down to 1.0 kPa CO2 partial pressure. Experiments conducted on 13 solvent systems under atmospheric conditions revealed the solvents absorption and desorption characteristics and these were compared with 1.0 M, 2.5 M, 5.0 M and 10.0 M MEA. Results showed that absorption or stripping data alone were not sufficient in making robust solvent selection decisions, and that combined data analysis was necessary. 1.0 M tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA) and 5.0 M MEA showed the best performance in terms of absorption rate. 1.5 M Bis-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) amine (TMBPA) was easy to desorb, has high absorption capacity; and when promoted it showed the best performance in terms of CO2 carrying capacity. At the test conditions, 1.5 M TMBPA promoted with 1.0 M PZ showed the best potential for efficient CO2 removal at reduced cost of all systems tested. Its cyclic capacity in mol CO2/mol amine was found to be 70% higher than that of 5 M MEA.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Climate change has been associated with increased emissions of carbon dioxide from anthropogenic sources and is identified as the most important contributing green house gas (Pachauri and Reisinger, 2007). It is thus pertinent that carbon dioxide emissions from combustion exhaust gases must be reduced. Many technologies exist today for separation and capture of CO2 from gas streams (Rao and Rubin, 2002) with absorption by amine based solvents being the most common for CO2 removal from combustion exhaust gases. Commonly used amine absorbents include monoethanolamine (MEA) and KS1 by Mitsubishi Heavy Industries. Efficient absorption has its challenges mainly from high energy demand of the process, being linked to the characteristics of the solvent used in the process. Development of more efficient solvents is thus crucial in reducing the cost of CO2 capture. Different factors affect the efficiency of a solvent for carbon dioxide capture and these include solvent solubility, its vapour pressure, molecular weight and foaming tendency, degradation and corrosion properties. Others are reaction kinetics, heat of reaction and regeneration energy requirement as well as the cyclic capacity. Environmental factors must also be considered seriously, so it is obvious that selection of the best solvent is not trivial.
texts.text: Climate change has been associated with increased emissions of carbon dioxide from anthropogenic sources and is identified as the most important contributing green house gas (Pachauri and Reisinger, 2007). It is thus pertinent that carbon dioxide emissions from combustion exhaust gases must be reduced. Many technologies exist today for separation and capture of CO2 from gas streams (Rao and Rubin, 2002) with absorption by amine based solvents being the most common for CO2 removal from combustion exhaust gases. Commonly used amine absorbents include monoethanolamine (MEA) and KS1 by Mitsubishi Heavy Industries. Efficient absorption has its challenges mainly from high energy demand of the process, being linked to the characteristics of the solvent used in the process. Development of more efficient solvents is thus crucial in reducing the cost of CO2 capture. Different factors affect the efficiency of a solvent for carbon dioxide capture and these include solvent solubility, its vapour pressure, molecular weight and foaming tendency, degradation and corrosion properties. Others are reaction kinetics, heat of reaction and regeneration energy requirement as well as the cyclic capacity. Environmental factors must also be considered seriously, so it is obvious that selection of the best solvent is not trivial.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This work aims at finding new solvents with high potential for low energy demand in the CO2 absorption process by analysis of the combined absorption-desorption properties of pre-selected solvents based on rapid screening experiments. The performance of the new solvents are compared with MEA. Solvent pre-selection focused mostly on solvents with a potential for high absorption capacity and fast reaction kinetics. These features were estimated using structure and p K a considerations of the solvents. Overall, an increase in absorption capacity with increase in chain length between different functional groups in alkanolamines is found (Singh et al., 2007). Also, an increase in alkyl groups and increased chain length between amine functional groups increases p K a. Bis-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) amine TMBPA has 3 amine sites (one secondary and two tertiary) indicating a potential for a high absorption capacity of 3 mol CO2/mol amine. A loading of 2.9 mol CO2/mol was achieved by Dallos et al. (2001). Two tertiary amine sites could probably result in lower reaction
texts.text: This work aims at finding new solvents with high potential for low energy demand in the CO2 absorption process by analysis of the combined absorption-desorption properties of pre-selected solvents based on rapid screening experiments. The performance of the new solvents are compared with MEA. Solvent pre-selection focused mostly on solvents with a potential for high absorption capacity and fast reaction kinetics. These features were estimated using structure and p K a considerations of the solvents. Overall, an increase in absorption capacity with increase in chain length between different functional groups in alkanolamines is found (Singh et al., 2007). Also, an increase in alkyl groups and increased chain length between amine functional groups increases p K a. Bis-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) amine TMBPA has 3 amine sites (one secondary and two tertiary) indicating a potential for a high absorption capacity of 3 mol CO2/mol amine. A loading of 2.9 mol CO2/mol was achieved by Dallos et al. (2001). Two tertiary amine sites could probably result in lower reaction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: rate (Versteeg et al., 1996), but presence of one secondary amine site and longer chains (3 carbon atoms) between the secondary and tertiary sites in this solvent system, give better possibilities for improved kinetics than would be expected for a tertiary amine. Because TMBPA has two tertiary amine sites it was suspected that the reaction rate could be low, in particular at high CO2 loadings, and it was decided to activate it with promoters which could act as a shuttle, reacting rapidly with CO2 to reduce mass-transfer resistance and facilitate fast transport of CO2 into the liquid phase (Versteeg et al., 1996; Xu et al., 1992). Mixing a primary or secondary amine with a tertiary amine, could improve the CO2 selectivity in the presence of H2S as well as reduce regeneration cost (Chakravarty et al., 1985). Promoters selected were 2,2-dimethyl-1,3-propanediamine (DMPDA), 3-(methylamino)propylamine (MAPA), monoethanolamine (MEA) and piperazine (PZ). Structure and p K a considerations for other systems tested in this work have been described in earlier work (Aronu et al., 2009).
texts.text: rate (Versteeg et al., 1996), but presence of one secondary amine site and longer chains (3 carbon atoms) between the secondary and tertiary sites in this solvent system, give better possibilities for improved kinetics than would be expected for a tertiary amine. Because TMBPA has two tertiary amine sites it was suspected that the reaction rate could be low, in particular at high CO2 loadings, and it was decided to activate it with promoters which could act as a shuttle, reacting rapidly with CO2 to reduce mass-transfer resistance and facilitate fast transport of CO2 into the liquid phase (Versteeg et al., 1996; Xu et al., 1992). Mixing a primary or secondary amine with a tertiary amine, could improve the CO2 selectivity in the presence of H2S as well as reduce regeneration cost (Chakravarty et al., 1985). Promoters selected were 2,2-dimethyl-1,3-propanediamine (DMPDA), 3-(methylamino)propylamine (MAPA), monoethanolamine (MEA) and piperazine (PZ). Structure and p K a considerations for other systems tested in this work have been described in earlier work (Aronu et al., 2009).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Screening experiments were carried out for 13 solvent systems; 1.0 M, 2.5 M, 5.0 M and 10.0 M MEA; 0.42 M DIHEP (N,N ′ -di-(2-hydroxyethyl)piperazine) + 0.58 M HEP (N-2-hydroxyethylpiperazine); 2.5 M AMP (2-amino-2-methyl1-propanol); 2.5 M AMP + 0.5 M PZ (piperazine); 1.5 M TMBPA (Bis-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) amine); 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M DMPDA (Neopentandiamin); 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M MAPA (3(methylamino)propylamine); 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M MEA; 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M PZ and 1.0 M TEPA (tetraethylenepentamine). The amine absorbents used, with their purities, were obtained as follows: MEA (Sigma-Aldrich, purity ≥ 99.9 mass%); TMBPA (Aldrich, purity > 97 mass %); TEPA (Fluka, purity ∼ 85 mass%); DMPDA (BASF, purity > 97.5 mass%), while MAPA (purity > 99 mass %); PZ (anhydrous 99%); AMP (purity > 99 mass%); DIHEP (purity > 99 mass%), HEP (purity > 98.5 mass%) were all obtained from Acros Organics. The solvents were used without further purification. All solution samples were prepared with deionized water. The CO2 (purity > 99.99 mol%) and N2 (purity > 99.99 mol%) used were obtained from AGA Gas GmbH.
texts.text: Screening experiments were carried out for 13 solvent systems; 1.0 M, 2.5 M, 5.0 M and 10.0 M MEA; 0.42 M DIHEP (N,N ′ -di-(2-hydroxyethyl)piperazine) + 0.58 M HEP (N-2-hydroxyethylpiperazine); 2.5 M AMP (2-amino-2-methyl1-propanol); 2.5 M AMP + 0.5 M PZ (piperazine); 1.5 M TMBPA (Bis-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) amine); 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M DMPDA (Neopentandiamin); 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M MAPA (3(methylamino)propylamine); 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M MEA; 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M PZ and 1.0 M TEPA (tetraethylenepentamine). The amine absorbents used, with their purities, were obtained as follows: MEA (Sigma-Aldrich, purity ≥ 99.9 mass%); TMBPA (Aldrich, purity > 97 mass %); TEPA (Fluka, purity ∼ 85 mass%); DMPDA (BASF, purity > 97.5 mass%), while MAPA (purity > 99 mass %); PZ (anhydrous 99%); AMP (purity > 99 mass%); DIHEP (purity > 99 mass%), HEP (purity > 98.5 mass%) were all obtained from Acros Organics. The solvents were used without further purification. All solution samples were prepared with deionized water. The CO2 (purity > 99.99 mol%) and N2 (purity > 99.99 mol%) used were obtained from AGA Gas GmbH.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Absorption and desorption experiments were carried out using a rapid screening apparatus designed to operate at atmospheric conditions and up to 80 ◦ C. The method involves absorption of CO2 with solvent at 40 ◦ C up to 9.5 kPa CO2 partial pressure and stripping of the same solvent at 80 ◦ C down to 1.0 kPa gas phase CO2 concentration. This method is used to acquire first hand knowledge on the behaviour of each solvent system in an absorption process. The absorption experiments were performed as described in Ma'mun et al. (2007). However, the apparatus was modified to allow stripping operations (Aronu et al., 2009, 2010). This gives a fast relative comparison of absorption rate and absorption capacity as well as the stripping rate and possible lean loading, and thus enables estimation of the cyclic capacity of each solvent. Apart from these factors, other properties such as foaming, precipitation and possible discolouration upon CO2
texts.text: Absorption and desorption experiments were carried out using a rapid screening apparatus designed to operate at atmospheric conditions and up to 80 ◦ C. The method involves absorption of CO2 with solvent at 40 ◦ C up to 9.5 kPa CO2 partial pressure and stripping of the same solvent at 80 ◦ C down to 1.0 kPa gas phase CO2 concentration. This method is used to acquire first hand knowledge on the behaviour of each solvent system in an absorption process. The absorption experiments were performed as described in Ma'mun et al. (2007). However, the apparatus was modified to allow stripping operations (Aronu et al., 2009, 2010). This gives a fast relative comparison of absorption rate and absorption capacity as well as the stripping rate and possible lean loading, and thus enables estimation of the cyclic capacity of each solvent. Apart from these factors, other properties such as foaming, precipitation and possible discolouration upon CO2

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This work compares solvents using molar concentrations of MEA as base solvent. It is, however, not optimal to test all solvents at the same molar concentration due to molecular weight and/or viscosity. To provide a broad basis for comparison, different concentrations of MEA, 1.0 M, 2.5 M, 5.0 M and 10.0 M MEA were tested. Thus, a comparison basis in terms of molar and/or weight composition was obtained, and also made it possible to see concentration effects. In general, we compared 2.5 M solutions of each solvent or solvent mixture with 5.0 M MEA and at same time tried using concentrations not too far from 30 wt% solutions. Due to high molecular weight and viscosity of TMBPA and TEPA, concentrations of 1.5 M and 1.0 M, respectively were used. DIHEP and HEP were found to be very expensive solvents and a mixture with the same molar concentrations as proposed by Hakka and Ouimet (2004), i.e. 0.42 M DIHEP and 0.58 M HEP (total of 1 M), was used. Since different concentrations of MEA were tested it was possible to compare all the solvents.
texts.text: This work compares solvents using molar concentrations of MEA as base solvent. It is, however, not optimal to test all solvents at the same molar concentration due to molecular weight and/or viscosity. To provide a broad basis for comparison, different concentrations of MEA, 1.0 M, 2.5 M, 5.0 M and 10.0 M MEA were tested. Thus, a comparison basis in terms of molar and/or weight composition was obtained, and also made it possible to see concentration effects. In general, we compared 2.5 M solutions of each solvent or solvent mixture with 5.0 M MEA and at same time tried using concentrations not too far from 30 wt% solutions. Due to high molecular weight and viscosity of TMBPA and TEPA, concentrations of 1.5 M and 1.0 M, respectively were used. DIHEP and HEP were found to be very expensive solvents and a mixture with the same molar concentrations as proposed by Hakka and Ouimet (2004), i.e. 0.42 M DIHEP and 0.58 M HEP (total of 1 M), was used. Since different concentrations of MEA were tested it was possible to compare all the solvents.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It should be noted that the comparison of the experimental data is semi-quantitative since there is no guarantee that the bubble structure, and thus the gas-liquid interfacial area, will be exactly the same in all experiments. However, the superficial gas velocities were the same, so the differences would arise mainly due to interfacial tension, bubble coalescence properties and viscosity, see Ma'mun et al. (2007).
texts.text: It should be noted that the comparison of the experimental data is semi-quantitative since there is no guarantee that the bubble structure, and thus the gas-liquid interfacial area, will be exactly the same in all experiments. However, the superficial gas velocities were the same, so the differences would arise mainly due to interfacial tension, bubble coalescence properties and viscosity, see Ma'mun et al. (2007).

texts.orig: The absorption rates versus loading plots for 1.0 M, 2.5 M, 5.0 M and 10.0 M MEA and the other amine systems are presented in Fig. 1a and b. Fig. 1a shows that, based on loading (mol CO2/mol amine), 1.0 M TEPA has a distinctively higher absorption rate than the other solvents, and also attained the highest loading of 1.916 mol CO2/mol. 1.5 M TMBPA also shows high absorption capacity and reasonably high absorption rate considering that it has two tertiary and one secondary amine groups. TMBPA promoted with PZ and MAPA shows higher absorption rate in the whole loading range than all MEA concentrations, and also achieves higher loading. High cost of DIHEP and HEP did not allow tests to be carried out at high concentrations, how-

Its rate could be improved significantly by activating with 0.5 M PZ as also seen in Fig. 1a.
texts.text: ever, the mixture proposed by Hakka and Ouimet (2004), i.e. 0.42 M DIHEP and 0.58 M HEP (total of 1 M) tested showed both lower rate and capacity than 1.0 M MEA (see Fig. 1a). 2.5 M AMP showed the lowest initial rate. Its rate could be improved significantly by activating with 0.5 M PZ as also seen in Fig. 1a.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An industrially more interesting way of evaluating the solvents is studying the absorption rate versus total amount of CO2 absorbed in solution (mol CO2 per litre solution) as given in Fig. 1b. This gives a completely different perspective on the solvents performances. It shows that 10.0 M MEA maintains the highest absorption rate and absorbs the largest amount of CO2 per volume compared to all the solvents. The only exception being 1.0 M TEPA which has a slightly higher initial absorption rate up to a concentration level of about 0.7 mol CO2/L. The reason for this change is of course because solvent CO2 absorption capacity, in mol CO2 per litre solution, is a function of the amine, or amine group, molar concentration (Kohl and Nielsen, 1997). An increase in molar concentration results in increasing solvent absorption capacity as seen by the increase in MEA concentration from 1.0 M, 2.5 M, 5 M and 10 M. It is therefore expected that increased concentrations of TEPA and TMBPA will increase the respective system absorption capacities. However, industrial use of these systems could be limited by associated problems caused by e.g. high viscosity and foaming. Initial screening experiments were conducted where half of the initial solvent concentrations were used, and the results showed less foaming. Solubility might also be an issue as for the high molecular weight solvents; the weight percent may become very high, thereby effectively reducing the amount of water present and
texts.text: An industrially more interesting way of evaluating the solvents is studying the absorption rate versus total amount of CO2 absorbed in solution (mol CO2 per litre solution) as given in Fig. 1b. This gives a completely different perspective on the solvents performances. It shows that 10.0 M MEA maintains the highest absorption rate and absorbs the largest amount of CO2 per volume compared to all the solvents. The only exception being 1.0 M TEPA which has a slightly higher initial absorption rate up to a concentration level of about 0.7 mol CO2/L. The reason for this change is of course because solvent CO2 absorption capacity, in mol CO2 per litre solution, is a function of the amine, or amine group, molar concentration (Kohl and Nielsen, 1997). An increase in molar concentration results in increasing solvent absorption capacity as seen by the increase in MEA concentration from 1.0 M, 2.5 M, 5 M and 10 M. It is therefore expected that increased concentrations of TEPA and TMBPA will increase the respective system absorption capacities. However, industrial use of these systems could be limited by associated problems caused by e.g. high viscosity and foaming. Initial screening experiments were conducted where half of the initial solvent concentrations were used, and the results showed less foaming. Solubility might also be an issue as for the high molecular weight solvents; the weight percent may become very high, thereby effectively reducing the amount of water present and

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The solvents attained different loadings and absorbed different amounts of CO2 in solution. Desorption tests at 80 ◦ C gives an indication of the level achievable for lean loading and thereby the CO2 carrying capacity for these solvent systems under the experimental conditions. Fig. 1c shows stripping rate versus loading (mol CO2/mol amine) while in Fig. 1d stripping rate is plotted against amount of CO2 in solution, mol CO2/L. It can be observed from the desorption plots that, as expected, stripping rate decreases from high to low loading opposite to the absorption rate which decreases from low to high loading. In Fig. 1c it is seen that the lowest lean loading of about 0.08 mol CO2/mol amine was attained with the 2.5 M AMP and 0.42 M DIHEP + 0.58 M HEP systems, showing the high desorption ability of these solvents. It also shows that 1.5 M TMBPA has the ability both to attain low lean loading (0.31 mol CO2/mol amine) and high rich loading (1.62 mol CO2/mol amine, Fig. 1a) while 1.0 M TEPA which gave the highest rich loading (1.92 mol CO2/mol amine) only could reach a lean loading of 0.81 mol CO2/mol amine under the same conditions. This gives an indication of the good desorption characteristics of TMBPA and its potential for lower regeneration energy demand than TEPA. This behaviour could be related to the presence of fast reacting, carbamate forming primary and secondary amines sites in TEPA. In the same figure the relatively narrow range between rich and lean loading for the MEA solutions can be noticed.
texts.text: The solvents attained different loadings and absorbed different amounts of CO2 in solution. Desorption tests at 80 ◦ C gives an indication of the level achievable for lean loading and thereby the CO2 carrying capacity for these solvent systems under the experimental conditions. Fig. 1c shows stripping rate versus loading (mol CO2/mol amine) while in Fig. 1d stripping rate is plotted against amount of CO2 in solution, mol CO2/L. It can be observed from the desorption plots that, as expected, stripping rate decreases from high to low loading opposite to the absorption rate which decreases from low to high loading. In Fig. 1c it is seen that the lowest lean loading of about 0.08 mol CO2/mol amine was attained with the 2.5 M AMP and 0.42 M DIHEP + 0.58 M HEP systems, showing the high desorption ability of these solvents. It also shows that 1.5 M TMBPA has the ability both to attain low lean loading (0.31 mol CO2/mol amine) and high rich loading (1.62 mol CO2/mol amine, Fig. 1a) while 1.0 M TEPA which gave the highest rich loading (1.92 mol CO2/mol amine) only could reach a lean loading of 0.81 mol CO2/mol amine under the same conditions. This gives an indication of the good desorption characteristics of TMBPA and its potential for lower regeneration energy demand than TEPA. This behaviour could be related to the presence of fast reacting, carbamate forming primary and secondary amines sites in TEPA. In the same figure the relatively narrow range between rich and lean loading for the MEA solutions can be noticed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Fig. 1d the stripping rates are plotted for the amines showing the amount of CO2 removed in mol/L solution. The curves show that 10.0 M MEA absorbed the highest amount of CO2 (4.74 mol CO2/L), however, only a small amount of this could be desorbed under the conditions used, resulting in 2.73 mol CO2/L solution at the lean end. The curves for 2.5 M AMP and 1.5 M TMBPA clearly show that low lean CO2 concentrations are obtainable also for systems with high rich loadings. The blend of 2.5 M AMP with 0.5 M PZ and all blends of 1.5 M TMBPA and promoters (excluding 1.0 M MAPA and 1.0 M DMPDA promoters) equally show low concentrations of CO2 at the lean end. The plots show that the lowest lean end concentration of CO2 is achieved in 0.42 M DIHEP + 0.58 M HEP (total of 1 M). This gives an indication of high desorption ability of this mixture when compared to 1.0 M MEA and 1.0 M TEPA. On the other hand, this solvent has the lowest rich concentration of CO2 in solution.
texts.text: In Fig. 1d the stripping rates are plotted for the amines showing the amount of CO2 removed in mol/L solution. The curves show that 10.0 M MEA absorbed the highest amount of CO2 (4.74 mol CO2/L), however, only a small amount of this could be desorbed under the conditions used, resulting in 2.73 mol CO2/L solution at the lean end. The curves for 2.5 M AMP and 1.5 M TMBPA clearly show that low lean CO2 concentrations are obtainable also for systems with high rich loadings. The blend of 2.5 M AMP with 0.5 M PZ and all blends of 1.5 M TMBPA and promoters (excluding 1.0 M MAPA and 1.0 M DMPDA promoters) equally show low concentrations of CO2 at the lean end. The plots show that the lowest lean end concentration of CO2 is achieved in 0.42 M DIHEP + 0.58 M HEP (total of 1 M). This gives an indication of high desorption ability of this mixture when compared to 1.0 M MEA and 1.0 M TEPA. On the other hand, this solvent has the lowest rich concentration of CO2 in solution.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The two loading units, mol CO2/mol amine and mol CO2/L discussed in Sections 3.1 and 3.2 for Fig. 1 show that it is necessary to look at the absorption and desorption plots not only from one perspective because different units of loading gives different impressions with respect to solvent performance. As discussed, e.g. in Fig. 1a (unit is mol CO2/mol amine) 1 M TEPA appears to be the most promising solvent while in Fig. 1b (unit is mol CO2/L), 10 M MEA appear to be most promising. Looking at only one concentration unit appears to limit detailed analysis of solvent performance.
texts.text: The two loading units, mol CO2/mol amine and mol CO2/L discussed in Sections 3.1 and 3.2 for Fig. 1 show that it is necessary to look at the absorption and desorption plots not only from one perspective because different units of loading gives different impressions with respect to solvent performance. As discussed, e.g. in Fig. 1a (unit is mol CO2/mol amine) 1 M TEPA appears to be the most promising solvent while in Fig. 1b (unit is mol CO2/L), 10 M MEA appear to be most promising. Looking at only one concentration unit appears to limit detailed analysis of solvent performance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: During desorption tests some of the solvents flashed creating a fast upward flow of solvent-gas mixture from the reactor into the condenser as soon as N2 was introduced to start the strip- ping process. Flashing was observed in AMP and TMBPA and in all their mixtures as well as in TEPA. Strongest flashing was observed in 2.5 M AMP + 0.5 M PZ, 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M PZ, 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M MAPA and 1.0 M TEPA. Three factors are considered to be likely contributors to this phenomenon; high CO2 desorption potential, foaming tendency and viscosity. Flashing of the AMP and TMBPA solutions is believed to be due to their high desorption potentials, or in other words, high equilibrium CO2 pressure already at 80 ◦ C. Foaming combined with high viscosity could also contribute to flashing in the TMBPA and TEPA solutions. One hypothesis was that foaming could be part contributor to the flashing problems. A foam inhibitor was added in these cases and flashing was effectively suppressed. Foam inhibitor was also used during the absorption tests of 1.5 M TMBPA, thus in reality this system could have a somewhat higher absorption rate than observed in Fig. 1a and b because the foam inhibitor may reduce the active gas/liquid interfacial area.
texts.text: During desorption tests some of the solvents flashed creating a fast upward flow of solvent-gas mixture from the reactor into the condenser as soon as N2 was introduced to start the strip- ping process. Flashing was observed in AMP and TMBPA and in all their mixtures as well as in TEPA. Strongest flashing was observed in 2.5 M AMP + 0.5 M PZ, 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M PZ, 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M MAPA and 1.0 M TEPA. Three factors are considered to be likely contributors to this phenomenon; high CO2 desorption potential, foaming tendency and viscosity. Flashing of the AMP and TMBPA solutions is believed to be due to their high desorption potentials, or in other words, high equilibrium CO2 pressure already at 80 ◦ C. Foaming combined with high viscosity could also contribute to flashing in the TMBPA and TEPA solutions. One hypothesis was that foaming could be part contributor to the flashing problems. A foam inhibitor was added in these cases and flashing was effectively suppressed. Foam inhibitor was also used during the absorption tests of 1.5 M TMBPA, thus in reality this system could have a somewhat higher absorption rate than observed in Fig. 1a and b because the foam inhibitor may reduce the active gas/liquid interfacial area.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Some degree of colour change was observed in several solvents during the experiments. 1.0 M TEPA showed changed from colourless to yellow solution during stripping while 1.5 M TMBPA was found to changed from light yellow to light brown after stripping. 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M MEA changed from light yellow to brownish orange and 10.0 M MEA changed to yellow after absorption and stripping. These colour changes could indicate degradation tendencies, but this may not be the reason.
texts.text: Some degree of colour change was observed in several solvents during the experiments. 1.0 M TEPA showed changed from colourless to yellow solution during stripping while 1.5 M TMBPA was found to changed from light yellow to light brown after stripping. 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M MEA changed from light yellow to brownish orange and 10.0 M MEA changed to yellow after absorption and stripping. These colour changes could indicate degradation tendencies, but this may not be the reason.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The performance of each solvent system was assessed by using a selection of quantifying criteria, as given by Table 1. The criteria considered are: cyclic capacity (defined in mol CO2/mol amine), percent of the rich amine CO2 content removed during desorption (80 ◦ C), CO2 carrying capacity per litre solution, and in addition the rate quantifiers; absorption rate at lean conditions and at 90% of equilibrium rich loading. Using all these parameters together allows a true comparative analysis of the solvents. The absorption-desorption cycle calculation procedure used in this work is given in Section 2.3; see also Aronu et al. (2010). Table 1 shows a summary of the results from the absorption-desorption experiments.
texts.text: The performance of each solvent system was assessed by using a selection of quantifying criteria, as given by Table 1. The criteria considered are: cyclic capacity (defined in mol CO2/mol amine), percent of the rich amine CO2 content removed during desorption (80 ◦ C), CO2 carrying capacity per litre solution, and in addition the rate quantifiers; absorption rate at lean conditions and at 90% of equilibrium rich loading. Using all these parameters together allows a true comparative analysis of the solvents. The absorption-desorption cycle calculation procedure used in this work is given in Section 2.3; see also Aronu et al. (2010). Table 1 shows a summary of the results from the absorption-desorption experiments.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It shows that the highest rich loading in mol CO2/mol amine was attained by 1.0 M TEPA and lowest lean loading was achieved in 0.42 M DIHEP + 0.58 M HEP while 1.5 M TMBPA has the highest cyclic capacity. Highest percent CO2 removal per cycle was found in 2.5 M AMP. This underlines the high equilibrium temperature sensitivity for this system, being one of the key factors for reduced energy demand in the process. All promoted 1.5 M TMBPA show high carry capacity with the highest value found in 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M PZ. 1.0 M TEPA has the highest absorption rate at lean loading while 5.0 M MEA showed the highest absorption rate at 90% equilibrium approach. High absorption rate at close to equilibrium loading is important in order to have a close equilibrium approach at the bottom of the absorber.
texts.text: It shows that the highest rich loading in mol CO2/mol amine was attained by 1.0 M TEPA and lowest lean loading was achieved in 0.42 M DIHEP + 0.58 M HEP while 1.5 M TMBPA has the highest cyclic capacity. Highest percent CO2 removal per cycle was found in 2.5 M AMP. This underlines the high equilibrium temperature sensitivity for this system, being one of the key factors for reduced energy demand in the process. All promoted 1.5 M TMBPA show high carry capacity with the highest value found in 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M PZ. 1.0 M TEPA has the highest absorption rate at lean loading while 5.0 M MEA showed the highest absorption rate at 90% equilibrium approach. High absorption rate at close to equilibrium loading is important in order to have a close equilibrium approach at the bottom of the absorber.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Analyses results show that it is difficult to make any selection decision on the most promising solvent by consideration of a single character of the solvents since different solvents have different unique characteristics. More consistent solvent selection decisions require combined analyses of these

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1. Performance in terms of combined CO2 absorption rate and capacity
texts.text: 4.1. Performance in terms of combined CO2 absorption rate and capacity

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 2 gives a picture of the relative performance of the solvents with regard to capacity and rate and shows plots for amine absorption rates at lean loading as function of percent CO2 removed. It shows that 1.0 M TEPA followed by 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M PZ have the highest absorption rate for the lean solution, but that 1.0 M TEPA has a lower relative desorption potential. 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M PZ shows both high absorption rates at lean loading and high relative desorption ability.
texts.text: Fig. 2 gives a picture of the relative performance of the solvents with regard to capacity and rate and shows plots for amine absorption rates at lean loading as function of percent CO2 removed. It shows that 1.0 M TEPA followed by 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M PZ have the highest absorption rate for the lean solution, but that 1.0 M TEPA has a lower relative desorption potential. 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M PZ shows both high absorption rates at lean loading and high relative desorption ability.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A more absolute comparison is given in Fig. 3. In Fig. 3a, amine absorption rate at lean loading is plotted against the CO2 removed per cycle for each solvent system. These plots clearly show that 1.5 M TMBPA with all its mixtures as well as 10.0 M MEA have particularly high carrying capacities compared to other amines. TMBPA and its family are of particular interest in this case considering their lower molar concentrations when compared to 5.0 and 10.0 M MEA. They show high CO2 carrying capacity with high desorption ability.
texts.text: A more absolute comparison is given in Fig. 3. In Fig. 3a, amine absorption rate at lean loading is plotted against the CO2 removed per cycle for each solvent system. These plots clearly show that 1.5 M TMBPA with all its mixtures as well as 10.0 M MEA have particularly high carrying capacities compared to other amines. TMBPA and its family are of particular interest in this case considering their lower molar concentrations when compared to 5.0 and 10.0 M MEA. They show high CO2 carrying capacity with high desorption ability.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The figure further shows that 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M PZ system has the best performance when absorption rate at lean loading is combined with solvent carrying capacity; 0.42 M DIHEP + 0.58 HEP has lower performance when compared with 1.0 M MEA and 1.0 M TEPA. It is also observed that 2.5 M AMP has the lowest absorption rate at lean loading. The reaction rate of the solvents upon approach to equilibrium is very important, as mentioned. In general the absorption rate is expected to decrease on equilibrium approach but a solvent with characteristics of fast reaction towards equilibrium is desired. Fig. 3b shows plots for absorption rate at 90% equilibrium loading against CO2 removed per cycle. The plot shows that 5.0 M MEA has the highest absorption rate

It is also observed that 2.5 M AMP has the lowest absorption rate at lean loading. The reaction rate of the solvents upon approach to equilibrium is very important, as mentioned. In general the absorption rate is expected to decrease on equilibrium approach but a solvent with characteristics of fast reaction towards equilibrium is desired. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2. Performance in terms of CO2 absorption rate
texts.text: 4.2. Performance in terms of CO2 absorption rate

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Overall solvent performance in terms of rate was deduced using a plot of absorption rate at lean loading against absorption rate at 90% equilibrium loading (Fig. 4a). This shows how fast the lean solvent will react when returned to the absorber and how far the rate will be maintained towards equilibrium. Solvents with best rate performance have potential of reducing absorber dimensions and thus capital cost as well as operating cost. The figure shows that 1.0 M TEPA and 5.0 M MEA shows overall best performance in terms of rate while 2.5 AMP has the poorest performance. 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M PZ maintained a comparatively high rate performance.
texts.text: Overall solvent performance in terms of rate was deduced using a plot of absorption rate at lean loading against absorption rate at 90% equilibrium loading (Fig. 4a). This shows how fast the lean solvent will react when returned to the absorber and how far the rate will be maintained towards equilibrium. Solvents with best rate performance have potential of reducing absorber dimensions and thus capital cost as well as operating cost. The figure shows that 1.0 M TEPA and 5.0 M MEA shows overall best performance in terms of rate while 2.5 AMP has the poorest performance. 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M PZ maintained a comparatively high rate performance.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.3. Performance in terms of CO2 carrying capacity
texts.text: 4.3. Performance in terms of CO2 carrying capacity

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A plot of amount of CO2 removed per cycle against percent CO2 removed in Fig. 4b gives an indication of the overall
texts.text: A plot of amount of CO2 removed per cycle against percent CO2 removed in Fig. 4b gives an indication of the overall

texts.label: text
texts.orig: performance of each solvent system in terms of CO2 carrying capacity as a comparative indication of how much of the CO2 absorbed by the solvent is finally removed. Solvents with high carrying capacity will reduce the solvent circulation rate. Together with a high desorption rate at 80 ◦ C they will have a potential for low regeneration energy requirement in a process, since increased CO2 carrying capacity lowers the regeneration energy by lowering the sensible heat requirement in the stripper.
texts.text: performance of each solvent system in terms of CO2 carrying capacity as a comparative indication of how much of the CO2 absorbed by the solvent is finally removed. Solvents with high carrying capacity will reduce the solvent circulation rate. Together with a high desorption rate at 80 ◦ C they will have a potential for low regeneration energy requirement in a process, since increased CO2 carrying capacity lowers the regeneration energy by lowering the sensible heat requirement in the stripper.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 4b shows that 1.5 M TMBPA and its mixtures have the highest CO2 carrying capacity with 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M PZ, 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M MEA and 1.5 M TMBPA having the best performance. MEA at its various concentrations show generally lower carrying capacity with the best performance for 10.0 M MEA.
texts.text: Fig. 4b shows that 1.5 M TMBPA and its mixtures have the highest CO2 carrying capacity with 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M PZ, 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M MEA and 1.5 M TMBPA having the best performance. MEA at its various concentrations show generally lower carrying capacity with the best performance for 10.0 M MEA.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M PZ appears to have the overall best performance of all the solvent systems tested and analyzed. In terms of absorption rate, it shows high initial rate, high rate at 90% equilibrium approach and also high absorption rate at lean loading. On the other hand, in terms of
texts.text: 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M PZ appears to have the overall best performance of all the solvent systems tested and analyzed. In terms of absorption rate, it shows high initial rate, high rate at 90% equilibrium approach and also high absorption rate at lean loading. On the other hand, in terms of

texts.label: text
texts.orig: absorption capacity, it attains a comparatively high loading of 1.231 mol CO2/mol amine, absorbs large amounts of CO2 per litre solution (3.076 mol CO2/L) and desorbs about 74% of the CO2 absorbed under the experimental conditions, thereby being the solvent with highest CO2 carrying capacity (2.277 mol CO2/L). Its cyclic capacity in mol CO2/mol amine and CO2 per litre solution per cycle is respectively 70% and 41% higher than for 5 M MEA. The system did not show any significant discolouration during the experiments indicating some degree of stability of the solvent. However, flashing and foaming were observed. Flashing could be a result of the high desorption ability of TMBPA, foaming of the solvent system or a combination of these. For 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M PZ the high desorption at 80 ◦ C shows a potential for reduced desorption energy while its high carrying capacity will result in lower solvent circulation rate. Together these factors will result in lower operating cost for the process while its high absorption rate performance could entail lower absorber dimensions which will reduce capital as well as operating cost of the process. This solvent system thus appears to have a high potential for more efficient CO2 removal at a lower cost.
texts.text: absorption capacity, it attains a comparatively high loading of 1.231 mol CO2/mol amine, absorbs large amounts of CO2 per litre solution (3.076 mol CO2/L) and desorbs about 74% of the CO2 absorbed under the experimental conditions, thereby being the solvent with highest CO2 carrying capacity (2.277 mol CO2/L). Its cyclic capacity in mol CO2/mol amine and CO2 per litre solution per cycle is respectively 70% and 41% higher than for 5 M MEA. The system did not show any significant discolouration during the experiments indicating some degree of stability of the solvent. However, flashing and foaming were observed. Flashing could be a result of the high desorption ability of TMBPA, foaming of the solvent system or a combination of these. For 1.5 M TMBPA + 1.0 M PZ the high desorption at 80 ◦ C shows a potential for reduced desorption energy while its high carrying capacity will result in lower solvent circulation rate. Together these factors will result in lower operating cost for the process while its high absorption rate performance could entail lower absorber dimensions which will reduce capital as well as operating cost of the process. This solvent system thus appears to have a high potential for more efficient CO2 removal at a lower cost.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A method for combined data analysis using absorption-desorption dot plots was developed and found very useful in solvent assessment from rapid screening experiments. A range of 13 different amine systems was tested. Bis-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) amine (TMBPA) and tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA) showed good potential as solvents for CO2 absorption. TEPA has both high absorption rate and capacity but lower desorption ability under the experimental conditions. TMBPA showed very high carrying capacity for CO2 and relatively good kinetics, but requires a promoter for increased absorption rate, particularly on approach to equilibrium. Promoting 1.5 M TMBPA with 1.0 M MEA and 1.0 M DMPDA showed higher foaming tendency while promoting with 1.0 M MAPA showed lower desorption degree. Promoting 1.5 M TMBPA with 1.0 M PZ was found to have the best overall performance in terms of potential for efficient CO2 removal at reduced cost of all the systems tested. Its cyclic capacity in mol CO2/mol amine was found 70% higher than 5 M MEA and it removes 41% more CO2 per cycle per litre solution than 5 M MEA at test conditions. High molecular weight could limit the use of these solvents at higher concentrations due to viscosity and potential foaming.
texts.text: A method for combined data analysis using absorption-desorption dot plots was developed and found very useful in solvent assessment from rapid screening experiments. A range of 13 different amine systems was tested. Bis-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) amine (TMBPA) and tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA) showed good potential as solvents for CO2 absorption. TEPA has both high absorption rate and capacity but lower desorption ability under the experimental conditions. TMBPA showed very high carrying capacity for CO2 and relatively good kinetics, but requires a promoter for increased absorption rate, particularly on approach to equilibrium. Promoting 1.5 M TMBPA with 1.0 M MEA and 1.0 M DMPDA showed higher foaming tendency while promoting with 1.0 M MAPA showed lower desorption degree. Promoting 1.5 M TMBPA with 1.0 M PZ was found to have the best overall performance in terms of potential for efficient CO2 removal at reduced cost of all the systems tested. Its cyclic capacity in mol CO2/mol amine was found 70% higher than 5 M MEA and it removes 41% more CO2 per cycle per litre solution than 5 M MEA at test conditions. High molecular weight could limit the use of these solvents at higher concentrations due to viscosity and potential foaming.
